channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: What’s Behind Broadway’s New Musical Drought?
item.description: Hollywood actors in starry plays, skittish investors and gate-keeping theater owners have all contributed to an unusually tough climate for song-and-dance shows.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (Play)
Midnight in the Garden of Good and Evil (Play)
item.media:credit: Tim Lahan

item.title: A Billionaire’s Basquiat Collection Goes Up at Pérez Art Museum Miami
item.description: Ken Griffin, the hedge fund manager, looks for artworks with a “wow” factor, never mind the number of zeros on the price tag. Ten of his Basquiats are on loan to the Pérez Art Museum Miami.
item.dc:creator: Brett Sokol and Ysa Pérez
item.category: Perez, Jorge M, Art Museum of Miami-Dade County
item.media:description: Art handlers with Basquiat’s “Pez Dispenser,” from 1984, at PAMM. The show represents the collector Ken Griffin’s coming out as a major player on the Miami art scene.

item.title: A Show for All Seasons: Henry Moore’s Art, Reborn in Open Air
item.description: At the Kew Royal Botanic Gardens in London, a massive outdoor exhibition reveals a lifelong dialogue in the sculptor’s work between nature and human creations.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge

item.title: When the World Cup Came to SoFi Stadium, He Got a Backyard View
item.description: SoFi Stadium, the $5 billion event venue near Los Angeles, is on global display for the soccer tournament. But for some who live close by, it is just another headache.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Jamie Lee Taete for The New York Times

item.title: An Artist Creates Moments for Play, and Solidarity
item.description: From the Queens Museum to Times Square, the British artist Sonia Boyce shows art intimately connected to community.
item.dc:creator: Aruna D’Souza
item.media:credit: Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York/DACS, London; Photograph by Olivia Lifungula for The New York Times

item.title: Rare Books on Sex Have Spiced Things Up at a Library Franklin Founded
item.description: The Library Company of Philadelphia, created in 1731 by Benjamin Franklin, has received a gift of 1,500 volumes about sexuality dating back to the 17th century.
item.dc:creator: Ralph Blumenthal and Caroline Gutman
item.media:description: Charles E. Rosenberg has been giving rare books to the Library Company of Philadelphia since the 1960s. His most recent gift was a collection of some 1,500 texts about sexuality.

item.title: ‘Henry VI,’ ‘Camping’ and 6 More Shows to See Now
item.description: New York in June is a bonanza of Off and Off Off Broadway productions. Here’s our guide to shows we recommend, many in the city’s coziest spaces (and closing soon).
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Can’t Stop Second-Screening? Netflix Is Counting on It.
item.description: A new game featuring the voices of Zoë Kravitz and Sadie Sink turns a user’s phone into a controller in an attempt to combine the first and second screen experience.
item.dc:creator: Megan Farokhmanesh

item.title: Clive Davis and Whitney Houston’s Successful and Tragic Story
item.description: The record industry titan guided Houston from a young star to worldwide phenomenon. But their story together was not without controversy.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Larry Busacca/WireImage, via Getty Images
item.media:description: In Whitney Houston, Clive Davis recognized the potential of a powerful mezzo-soprano as an instrument to reshape modern pop music.

item.title: Mourning After the Michael Jackson Movie? It Might Be ‘Michosis.’
item.description: Some Michael Jackson fans are experiencing deep, lingering grief after watching the biopic — a potent reminder that he is gone, they say.
item.dc:creator: Farah Fleurima
item.media:credit: Lionsgate
item.media:description: Jaafar Jackson as Michael Jackson in the recent biopic “Michael,” which has set off a wave of renewed mourning over the pop star’s death.

item.title: Lin-Manuel Miranda’s ‘Warriors’ Musical to Hit Broadway Next Spring
item.description: Miranda is co-writing the musical — his first since “Hamilton” — with Eisa Davis. It’s based on “The Warriors” film and novel.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Daniel Weiss for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lin-Manuel Miranda and Eisa Davis have jointly written the book, music and lyrics for their new musical, “Warriors.”

item.title: ‘The Sound of Music’ and ‘A Few Good Men’ Are Coming to Broadway
item.description: Lincoln Center Theater, basking in the glow of its Tony-winning “Ragtime” run, plans revivals of two more well-known titles this season.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Natasha Moustache/Getty Images

item.title: 7 Songs That Spun My Head Around
item.description: Hear a Rosalía tour highlight, forgotten new-wave from the 1970s and more.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: The New York Times
item.media:description: Rosalía’s dazzling Lux Tour hit Madison Square Garden last week.

item.title: Manhattan Borough President Gives $50 Million to N.Y.C. Arts Groups
item.description: In allocating the borough’s discretionary budget entirely to cultural projects, Brad Hoylman-Sigal said he wanted to send a message to President Trump about the need to keep arts funding.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.category: LaGuardia, Fiorello H, High School of Music and Performing Arts
Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture

item.title: Jimmy Fallon Teases Trump’s Great American State Fair
item.description: “There’s even a dunk tank with JD Vance and a drunk tank with Kash Patel,” Fallon said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: A Record Sotheby’s Auction Thrills London After a Brexit Hangover
item.description: British auction sales have declined by nearly half since 2015, but Wednesday’s results hint at a return to form for London as an international auction center.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Delight to Your Day
item.description: Naming the moon, Sometimes Island and laughing in real life.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: ‘The Moment,’ ‘Daddio’ and More Streaming Gems
item.description: A pair of unconventional takes to the life of a musician bookend this month’s under-the-radar recommendations for your subscription streaming services.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey
item.category: Swamp Dogg Gets His Pool Painted (Movie)

item.title: ‘In the Hand of Dante’ Review: A Not So Divine Folly
item.description: Not even a double dose of Oscar Isaac can rescue this fanciful, oversauced tale of stolen art and spiritual questioning.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: The Pursuit of Immortality in The Times
item.description: William McDonald, who recently retired as the obituaries editor after nearly two decades, shares how subjects are chosen to be remembered in the newspaper.
item.dc:creator: William McDonald

item.title: Josh Johnson Muses on the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool
item.description: “Hey, kids, remember you wanted to go to Disneyland? Instead, we’re going to go see the world’s largest kombucha!” Johnson said of the pool renovation as a tourist stop.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Lorcan O’Herlihy, Architect of Innovative Urban Housing, Dies at 66
item.description: Arguing that architecture is a “social act,” he built affordable homes on small lots in Los Angeles and elsewhere, with an eye toward smarter, more equitable design.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Michael Weschler for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lorcan O’Herlihy in 2000. “As global cities become denser and the need for housing greater, it is more critical than ever to design spaces that promote equity, human interaction and cultural evolution,” he said.

item.title: Artist Pulls Work From London Museum After Clash Over Churchill’s Legacy
item.description: A historian and others said that a video installation had incorrectly blamed Winston Churchill for a famine in colonial India.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Jeremie Souteyrat for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Portrait Gallery in London. A video installation called “Persistence” was taken down this week after a historian criticized a reference to Winston Churchill.

item.title: Up Late With Botticelli as the Uffizi Gets a Reset
item.description: The museum’s director, Simone Verde, is working long hours as he tries to remake the institution into a living encyclopedia.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo and Clara Vannucci
item.media:credit: Clara Vannucci for The New York Times

item.title: 2026 Jimmy Awards: With Bowen Yang as Host, Teen Winners Are Crowned
item.description: Students from Georgia and Arizona won the top prizes at the ceremony, which was hosted by Bowen Yang and celebrates excellence in high school musical theater.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Photos Behind Kennedy Center Tarps Show No Sign of Trump’s Name
item.description: Images circulated by an activist group reveal bare marble where President Trump’s name once resided. The Kennedy Center previously told a federal judge it had been removed.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Hands Off the Arts
item.media:description: In a sworn declaration this month, the Kennedy Center said it had removed the words “The Donald J. Trump and” from its facade.

item.title: A New ‘Odyssey’ Audiobook Puts the ‘A.I.’ in ‘Michael Caine’
item.description: The longtime Christopher Nolan collaborator isn’t in the director’s forthcoming Homeric adaptation. But a new audiobook sets Caine’s voice off on its own adventure.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Michal Cizek/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Michael Caine has licensed his voice to the A.I. company ElevenLabs.

item.title: Charles Hinman, Who Brought New Dimensions to Painting, Dies at 93
item.description: Starting in the early 1960s, the New York artist blurred the line between painting and sculpture with his shaped, protruding canvases.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Jack Mitchell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Mr. Hinman in front of his works in 1967. Unlike the standard flat rectangular painting, some of his featured undulating curves; others, contours like cut gems.

item.title: Plays, Musicals and Theater Festivals Across the U.S. Worth Traveling For
item.description: Across the country, audiences will find an abundance of Shakespeare, exciting new plays, and musicals and regional repertories in bucolic settings.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes and Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: New York Stage and Film Co
Muny (Municipal Theatre Association of St Louis)
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The opening moment of the play “As You Like It” at Hudson Valley Shakespeare in Garrison, N.Y.

item.title: Late Night Dunks on Trump for Hiring Greenwater Services
item.description: Jimmy Fallon poked fun at the name of the business under fire for turning the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool bright green: “Nailed it.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)
Last Week Tonight With John Oliver (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Clive Davis Knew to Let Patti Smith Have Free Rein
item.description: For Patti Smith, the best guidance was something that Davis rarely granted: free rein.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Ron Galella, Ltd./Ron Galella Collection, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Patti Smith and Clive Davis in 1993.

item.title: Carlos Santana, Patti Smith and Other Celebs Pay Tribute to Clive Davis
item.description: Davis “treated me with the same respect and kindness as a 22-year-old nobody as he did after all my success,” Springsteen said after the music executive’s death on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Leon Bennett/Getty Images for the Recording Academy
item.media:description: Clive Davis and Gladys Knight at a pre-Grammy Awards gala in 2024. “There will never be another Clive,” Knight said after Davis’s death.

item.title: 11 Essential Songs Shepherded by Clive Davis
item.description: The label boss, who died on Monday, had a passion for hits — especially ones he masterminded — during his formidable tenures at Columbia, Arista and J Records.
item.dc:creator: Rob Tannenbaum
item.category: If You Don't Know Me by Now (Song)
item.media:credit: Reed Saxon/Associated Press
item.media:description: After Clive Davis, right, assured Carlos Santana that “Smooth” would be the most important song on the album “Supernatural,” it spent three months at No. 1.

item.title: YouTube Stars Take Center Stage at Creative Artists Agency
item.description: For years, creators were on the fringes at Creative Artists Agency, a Hollywood talent behemoth. Now the agency is putting them center stage.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Michelle Groskopf for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trisha Paytas, a digital star, is part of CAA’s growing roster of creators.

item.title: Clive Davis, Music Industry Titan Who Signed Whitney Houston, Dies at 94
item.description: He rose from a midlevel position at Columbia Records to become one of music’s most powerful executives, shepherding stars like Barry Manilow and Whitney Houston.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Bryan Derballa for The New York Times
item.media:description: One of the few executives in music to become a household name, Clive Davis maintained a visible role as a starmaker for more than five decades.

item.title: Here’s What ‘the Most Important Mozart Discovery in Decades’ Sounds Like
item.description: A newly found notebook documenting the composer’s lessons with a student includes seven previously unknown compositions.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Arlo Brown
item.media:credit: Kenzo Tribouillard/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Pages from Mozart’s handwritten notebook at the National Library of France.

item.title: Cannibals, Lobotomies, Lethal Birds: A Tennessee Williams Opera
item.description: “Suddenly Last Summer,” the composer Courtney Bryan’s first opera, adapts Williams’s play for Bard SummerScape.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: Fisher, Richard B, Center for the Performing Arts at Bard College
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tina Benko as Mrs. Venable.

item.title: In the Choreographer Pioneer Winter’s World, Every Body Is a Dancing Body
item.description: Pioneer Winter’s works expand ideas about who gets to be a professional dancer. In “Apollo,” his muses are older dancers, who are like living archives.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer and Scott McIntyre
item.media:description: “Onstage, offstage, it’s really just about filling the world with dance,” Winter said, “and then seeing the ripple effects that creates.”

item.title: The National Trust Names New President
item.description: Brent Leggs will lead the preservation organization, which has been in the news recently for challenging the Trump administration’s ballroom and Kennedy Center projects.
item.dc:creator: Jane Margolies
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
African American Cultural Heritage Action Fund

item.title: Flight of the Conchords and the Perils of Reunions
item.description: Bret McKenzie and Jemaine Clement are routinely asked about performing their musical satires again. They always said no, until this year. What changed?
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Clement said, “One of my favorite things to do was when someone heckled us, we talk about why that hurt our feelings.”

item.title: ‘The Bear’ and 7 More Shows to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: The hit FX series reaches its fifth and final season. The BET Awards are live in Los Angeles.
item.dc:creator: Geordon Wollner
item.media:credit: FX
item.media:description: Jeremy Allen White, left, and Ayo Edebiri in Season 4 of “The Bear.”

item.title: ‘House of the Dragon’ Season 3 Premiere Recap: A Song of Water and Fire
item.description: The Dance of the Dragons got into full swing with no shortage of blood, flames and intrigue, much of it at sea.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:credit: Ollie Upton/HBO
item.media:description: The Sea Snake (Steve Toussaint) wasn’t expecting an attack from the Triarchy, or to come under friendly fire.

item.title: Margaret Kerry, Body and Soul of Disney’s Tinker Bell, Dies at 97
item.description: A film crew and illustrator meticulously documented her pirouettes and pouts, giving substance to a character depicted previously as a spotlight onstage.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub
item.media:credit: David Livingston/Getty Images
item.media:description: Margaret Kerry in 2018. Trained as a dancer, she became the model for the animated version of Tinker Bell in the 1953 Disney movie “Peter Pan.”

item.title: Mark Singer, Longtime Writer for The New Yorker, Dies at 75
item.description: He joined the magazine’s staff at 23. Among the subjects of his profiles were the magician Ricky Jay and a pre-politics Donald Trump.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:credit: Neilson Barnard/Getty Images for The New Yorker
item.media:description: Mark Singer in 2009 speaking at The New Yorker Festival. He began working at the magazine when he was 23 and was best known for his profiles.

item.title: ‘Toy Story 5’ Fuels Hollywood’s Hottest Summer Since 2019
item.description: The Disney-Pixar sequel was expected to make $160 million in North America over the weekend, lifting the summer box office to $1.85 billion to date.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: San Antonio Mayor Says Kanye West’s July 4 Concert There Should Be Canceled
item.description: The mayor’s appeal comes after several of the rapper’s shows were canceled in Europe. A planned concert in Tampa, Fla., has also raised concern.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Ramon Van Flymen/Agence France-Presse, via Anp/Afp Via Getty Images
item.media:description: Ye, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West, performing in the Netherlands this month.

item.title: Tom Dreesen, Comic Who Kept Sinatra’s Crowds Laughing, Dies at 86
item.description: He rose from a hardscrabble childhood to form a pioneering interracial comedy duo. He later spent years opening for Frank Sinatra.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.category: Comedy Store (Los Angeles, Calif, Comedy Club)
item.media:credit: Bryan Bedder/Getty Images
item.media:description: Tom Dreesen performing in 2004. He was Frank Sinatra’s opening act for 13 years and said, “From my vantage point, every night, there was no TV series, no radio show, no movie deal that compared to what I was experiencing.”

item.title: How Basel Went From Art to Art Basel
item.description: Basel’s art history predates the world’s most prominent art fair by a few centuries.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: With ‘Girls Like Girls,’ Hayley Kiyoko Turns Her Teenage Pain Into Art
item.description: The once-closeted star has reinvented her song “Girls Like Girls” as a best-selling Y.A. novel and a new theatrical film. It wasn’t easy.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer
item.media:credit: Jennelle Fong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hayley Kiyoko in Los Angeles. Her feature directing debut, “Girls Like Girls,” is in theaters.

item.title: For Heirs of Custer and Sitting Bull, a 150-Year-Old Battle Is Personal
item.description: As the anniversary of the Battle of the Little Bighorn approaches, relatives of the two men still grapple with the legacy of a contentious moment in U.S. history.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Will Warasila
item.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times

item.title: The Dad Movie Canon: Films That Define Dad Cinema
item.description: What is it about a certain kind of film that appeals to fathers? Our writer took a stab at defining Dad Cinema and rounding up its greatest hits.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey
item.category: Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World (Movie)
item.media:credit: Clockwise from top left: Michael Weinstein/Castle Rock Entertainment; The Mirisch Company; Orion Pictures; Stephen Vaughan/Warner Bros.; Paramount Pictures/Moviepix, via Getty Images; Stephen Vaughan/20th Century Fox; Warner Bros.

item.title: Claude Guillemot, Ubisoft Co-Founder, Is Dead After Plane Crash in France
item.description: Claude Guillemot, 69, founded the video game company, which was also known for the hit Far Cry, with his brothers in 1986. He was killed in western France.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor

item.title: Tyla, FKA Twigs: Songs to Know This Week
item.description: Uplift for Juneteenth from Allison Russell, tangled love songs from Tyla and FKA twigs, and more.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Tyla, who performed as part of the World Cup pregame ceremony, is back with new music.

item.title: The Newest Wing in the City’s Oldest Museum Celebrates Democracy
item.description: The New York Historical’s expansive Tang Wing highlights the role of protest for America’s 250th anniversary.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter

item.title: A Nearsighted Artist Changed Her View and Found Paradise
item.description: A nearsighted artist with a solitary streak stumbled on a world of cultural experiences and authentic connections after years of taking refuge in her bookshelves.
item.dc:creator: Rachelle Meyer

item.title: A Scandinavian Answer to John Singer Sargent Gets Her Due
item.description: Dismissed for decades by critics as a mere “fashion portraitist” of high-society ladies, Asta Norregaard is now the subject of a major new retrospective at Oslo’s National Museum.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Private collection
item.media:description: Norregaard’s 1892 portrait of Elisabeth Fearnley, the socialite wife of a shipping magnate.

item.title: ‘Obsession’ Is a Surprise Blockbuster. Who Gets the Profits?
item.description: As the breakout horror hit crosses $300 million at the global box office, its art director set off a debate about just compensation for crew members.
item.dc:creator: Malia Mendez

item.title: Under a Turtle Shell, a Stunning New Home for Shakespeare
item.description: After 38 years in a tent, Hudson Valley Shakespeare opens one of the most spectacular outdoor performance spaces in the country.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.media:credit: Mohamed Sadek for The New York Times

item.title: Steve-O Has Beef With Father Time
item.description: “It sucks for anybody to get old,” the “Jackass” stunt performer says, “but for Steve-O, I feel like it is categorically not OK.”
item.dc:creator: Kathryn Shattuck
item.media:credit: Unique Nicole/Getty Images for Paramount Pictures

item.title: Why ‘Toy Story’ is the Best Franchise Ever
item.description: The New York Times film critic Alissa Wilkinson explores how the ‘Toy Story’ films leverage layers of nostalgia as a recipe for success.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Edward Vega and Gabriel Blanco

item.title: How These High Schoolers Are Preparing for the 2026 Jimmy Awards
item.description: Each June, students from around the country come to New York for the Jimmy Awards. We tagged along for a day of their intensive musical-theater residency.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli and Dolly Faibyshev
item.media:credit: Dolly Faibyshev for The New York Times

item.title: James Burrows, Master of the TV Sitcom, Dies at 85
item.description: Beloved by actors, he helped create “Cheers” and directed more than 1,000 episodes of hit shows like “Taxi,” “Friends” and “The Big Bang Theory.”
item.dc:creator: Glenn Rifkin
item.category: The Mary Tyler Moore Show (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Burrows in 2023. He won 11 Emmy Awards and received 47 nominations in a career that spanned five decades.
